## Introduction {#sec-introduction}

The race to build artificial intelligence infrastructure is the defining capital allocation event of the 2020s.
Between 2023 and 2025, the leading AI laboratories (Anthropic, OpenAI, Google DeepMind, and their cloud infrastructure providers) committed over \$200 billion in 2024 alone, with combined capital expenditure from the four largest investors projected to reach \$660--690 billion in 2026 [@jassy2026confidence].
These investments are largely irreversible: a \$10 billion training cluster cannot be easily repurposed, and the competitive dynamics of AI development make delay potentially fatal.
Yet the fundamental uncertainty driving these decisions---whether and when AI capabilities will reach a transformative threshold---remains profoundly unresolved.

A frontier AI laboratory produces intelligence by combining two forms of compute. *Training compute* is used to optimize a neural network's parameters on massive datasets, yielding a model whose quality scales as a power law of compute invested [@kaplan2020scaling; @altman2025observations]. *Inference compute* runs the trained model to serve predictions and generate revenue. Both activities consume the same scarce hardware, purpose-built GPU clusters housed in multi-billion-dollar data centers, creating an intertemporal allocation problem with no close parallel in traditional capital budgeting.^[In practice, AI infrastructure investment also encompasses substantial expenditure on power generation and transmission---dedicated natural gas plants, long-term power purchase agreements, on-site substations---required to supply the hundreds of megawatts that a single training cluster demands. For tractability, I model all of these complementary investments as a single capital outlay in "compute capacity," with the cost function $I(K) = cK^\gamma$ absorbing the full cost of bringing a unit of capacity online, including its power supply.] The infrastructure is highly irreversible: facilities take 18--24 months to build, GPU architectures depreciate on similar cycles, and algorithmic breakthroughs can devalue installed capacity overnight. Executives frame the resulting risk calculus in starkly asymmetric terms. Alphabet CEO Sundar Pichai has argued that "the risk of underinvesting is dramatically greater than the risk of overinvesting" [@pichai2024risk], while Nvidia CEO Jensen Huang contends that agentic AI and reasoning require "easily 100x more" compute than anticipated just one year prior [@huang2025scaling]. The prize these investments target, what this paper models as a regime switch to high demand, is variously called "transformative AI" or "Artificial General Intelligence" (AGI): a step change in model capability sufficient to automate a broad swath of cognitive tasks and generate sustained, qualitatively higher demand for compute [@amodei2024machines]. Executive timeline estimates for this event range from "superintelligence in a few thousand days" [@altman2024intelligence] and 2026--2027 [@amodei2024machines] to "not with current architectures" [@lecun2025autoregressive], and it is precisely this dispersion in beliefs that the revealed beliefs methodology developed below is designed to exploit.

This paper develops a unified model of irreversible capacity investment under demand uncertainty that captures the distinctive features of AI infrastructure competition.
I combine real options theory [@mcdonald1986value; @dixit1994investment] with regime switching [@guo2005investment], oligopoly competition [@grenadier2002option; @huisman2015strategic], endogenous default risk [@leland1994corporate], and diminishing returns calibrated to empirical scaling laws [@kaplan2020scaling; @hoffmann2022training].
The model delivers three main contributions.

First, I place the training-inference allocation at the center of the investment problem.
Firms choose not only when to invest and how much capacity to install, but how to split that capacity between model training (which builds capabilities for the future) and inference serving (which generates revenue today).
This allocation interacts with a novel *endogenous regime switch* mechanism: firms' aggregate training compute contributes to the arrival of transformative AI, making the regime switch arrival rate $\tilde{\lambda}$ a function of industry investment decisions.
In the current (low) regime, revenue depends on inference capacity through a Tullock contest; in the post-adoption (high) regime, revenue depends on training quality.
This regime-specific competition creates a genuine strategic trade-off absent from standard capacity models: a firm that allocates too much to training sacrifices current revenue and risks default, while a firm that allocates too little sacrifices its competitive position in the transformative AI regime.

Second, I provide closed-form expressions for optimal investment triggers and capacity choice in a duopoly with default risk, and show that the interaction between training allocation and leverage creates a *faith-based survival* mechanism: optimism about transformative AI, backed by training investment, raises the effective revenue coefficient $A_{\text{eff}}$ and thereby lowers the default boundary---the hope of AGI literally keeps the firm alive.
The model produces closed-form investment triggers extending @huisman2015strategic to incorporate capacity choice, training allocation, diminishing returns, and endogenous default.

Third, I develop an illustrative *revealed beliefs* methodology that inverts the model to extract implied AI timeline beliefs from observable investment decisions.
Because all model parameters except the arrival rate $\lambda$ are either directly observable or calibratable from public data, one can back out each firm's implied $\lambda$ from its capital expenditure and training allocation---in the spirit of @samuelson1938note, extracting private information from choices rather than stated forecasts.
The training fraction $\hat{\phi}$ provides a second identifying moment beyond the CapEx/Revenue ratio, enabling a diagnostic check for model specification.
Calibrated to stylized versions of Anthropic, OpenAI, Google DeepMind, and CoreWeave, the inversion reveals a wide dispersion of implied beliefs; the training fraction diagnostic shows that firms with identical investment intensity but different training allocations (e.g., Anthropic vs. CoreWeave) reveal fundamentally different information about their beliefs.
I emphasize that this exercise is illustrative: the cardinal values are sensitive to calibration assumptions and data availability, and the most reliable inference is the *ordinal ranking* of beliefs across firm types and the approximate order-of-magnitude dispersion (see @sec-valuation for a full discussion of identification and sensitivity).

I also introduce the *AI investment dilemma*: the cost to a firm of investing according to a $\lambda$ that differs from its true belief. The asymmetry of this trade-off is captured by Anthropic CEO Dario Amodei's observation: "I could buy \$1 trillion of compute that starts at the end of 2027. If my revenue is not \$1 trillion dollars, if it's even \$800 billion, there's no force on Earth, there's no hedge on Earth that could stop me from going bankrupt" [@amodei2026cone].
If a firm is conservative (investing with $\lambda_{\text{invest}} < \lambda_{\text{true}}$), it invests too late and loses first-mover advantage; if aggressive ($\lambda_{\text{invest}} > \lambda_{\text{true}}$), it overbuilds and risks default.
The training channel amplifies this asymmetry: an overly optimistic firm over-allocates to training, sacrificing the inference revenue it needs to survive.
I quantify this trade-off across the parameter space, showing that the asymmetry is substantial: aggressive overinvestment carries higher downside risk than conservative underinvestment, rationalizing the extreme capital commitments observed in practice only if firms hold genuinely optimistic beliefs about AI timelines.

{{< include _literature.qmd >}}

The paper proceeds as follows.
@sec-model presents the model environment with training-inference allocation, endogenous regime switching, the single-firm benchmark, and the duopoly equilibrium with default risk.
@sec-extensions develops the $N$-firm game.
@sec-calibration describes the calibration to AI infrastructure firms.
@sec-valuation presents the revealed beliefs methodology, valuation analysis, and the AI investment dilemma.
@sec-discussion considers policy implications.
@sec-conclusion concludes.
